summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that any authenticated user can access any DAG run's logs and outputs by providing the dagRunId, regardless of ownership or user isolation.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction pipeline should demonstrate horizontal privilege escalation where User A (viewer role) can successfully access User B's (viewer role) DAG run logs and outputs.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built and launched the Dagu server, created two separate viewer users, User B created and executed a DAG with sensitive output, and User A successfully accessed User B's DAG run logs and outputs via HTTP 200 responses.
verification_method: The run script creates two users, User B runs a DAG with sensitive data, User A enumerates DAG run IDs and accesses User B's logs/outputs, verifying HTTP 200 status and presence of User B's sensitive data in responses.
evidence_produced_by_run: User A successfully authenticated with viewer role credentials
User A retrieved User B's DAG run ID from the list endpoint
GET /api/v1/dagruns/{name}/{id}/log returned HTTP 200 with User B's log content
GET /api/v1/dagruns/{name}/{id}/outputs returned HTTP 200 with User B's sensitive output data
No ownership verification or HTTP 403 error, confirming IDOR vulnerability

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the current HEAD, and builds the vulnerable Dagu binary inside the container.
